text:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have proven to be very effective for a variety of problems. From advanced quantitative reasoning to very advanced natural language understanding, they have proven themselves to be very effective at tackling very complex problems and thus aiding in the scientific discovery process. However, current state-of-the-art LLMs have the tendency to produce what are called “hallucinations” - statements that are true-sounding but false. In this paper, we introduce a new method called FunSearch, short for search in function space, that uses an evolutionary strategy in conjunction with a pre-trained LLM, and a systematic evaluator, and show that it outperforms current state-of-the-art LLM-based solvers.

On Microsoft Azure, we applied FunSearch to the cap set problem from extremal combinatorics. FunSearch produced new, record-breaking constructions of large cap sets for both finite-dimensional and asymptotic cases. In another application of FunSearch, we discovered new heuristics for the online bin packing problem, all of which are competitive with well-known standards. The cap set problem and online bin packing are respectively one of the classic open problems in extremal combinatorics and one of the simplest NP-hard packing problems. The first LLM to ever solve a long-standing open problem in mathematics is now demonstrated to be capable. In contrast to the conventional computational search for an exact solution, we produce instead the programs that produce solutions, hence generating logic instead of data. Our results not only advance a specific mathematical science but also lead to the production of solutions, the legitimacy of which can be certified using elementary computational means within the loop itself. The feedback loop involving human domain experts can then yield very rapidly, at a speed that allows the resulting programs to be put to practical use. Many open problems in the mathematical sciences seem to be impenetrable, yet are easily verifiable. We showed on Azure how to harness this inherent asymmetry to the full potential of LLMs, transforming them into credible scientific and algorithmic verifiable discovery tools.
